input: For a new Victorian Class 1 build at pre-start, what should I check before advising that the project is ready to start?
expected_output: A passing answer separates contract/admin readiness from NCC compliance. It should check that the major domestic building contract scope, plans/specifications, deposit and progress payment terms, domestic building insurance where required, building permit documents, approved drawings, and energy compliance pathway are available. It should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria contract guidance for contract/payment items and cite NCC/Victorian energy sources for 7-star or Whole-of-Home requirements. It must not say the work is compliant unless the permit and source documents support that.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: a major domestic building contract must be in writing for most work over $10,000 and must set out terms, work description, contract price, deposit and progress payments.

Victorian Government: NCC 2022 energy efficiency standards for new homes have a mandatory commencement date in Victoria of 1 May 2024, increasing minimum NatHERS thermal performance from 6 to 7 stars and introducing Whole-of-Home energy use budgets.
NCC 2022 Volume Two Part H6: energy efficiency covers building design/construction and energy use by key equipment.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria major domestic building contract

input: The contract is build all stages. At base/slab stage in Victoria, what should I look out for before a progress payment is treated as due?
expected_output: A passing answer should say base stage completion depends on the floor system, cite the Consumer Affairs Victoria building stages source, and explain that build-all-stages standard progress payments use 10% at base stage. It should also say required permit inspections and approved plans/specifications need to be checked, and it should not certify structural adequacy without RBS/engineering evidence.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: for a concrete floor, base stage is complete when the floor is complete; for suspended concrete slab, concrete footings are poured; for timber floors, completion depends on footings, stumps/piers/columns or base brickwork.
Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule: build all stages contracts generally use 10% at base, 15% at frame, 35% at lock-up and 25% at fixing.
VBA mandatory inspections guidance: mandatory notification stages are critical stages when building work must be inspected to assess compliance with the Act, Regulations and building permit.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule

input: For a build-to-lock-up contract, the builder has reached base stage. What payment percentage and inspection cautions should the advisor mention?
expected_output: A passing answer should identify this as a contract-stage/payment question, cite the build-to-lock-up standard payment schedule, and state that base stage is generally 20% for that contract type. It should caution that payment should be tied to stage completion under the contract and any required inspection outcome, not just a verbal claim. It should avoid withholding/payment legal advice beyond the cited source.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule: build to lock-up contracts list 20% at base stage and 25% at frame stage.

Consumer Affairs Victoria: before making each progress payment, check that work has passed any required inspection identified on the building permit, meets contract plans and specifications, and is complete.
VBA: the builder must notify the Relevant Building Surveyor when applicable mandatory notification stages are reached.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule

input: At frame stage for a Victorian new home, what should the advisor look out for before saying the frame milestone is complete?
expected_output: A passing answer should say frame stage is complete when the frame is completed and approved by a building surveyor, cite Consumer Affairs Victoria, and mention checking inspection outcome, approved plans, engineering/bracing tie-down documentation where retrieved, and permit conditions. It must not independently certify structural compliance.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: frame stage is complete when the frame is completed and approved by a building surveyor.
VBA mandatory inspections guidance: inspections provide for assessment of building work before further work can continue and are critical for structural, safety and amenity matters.
NCC Volume Two Section H includes Part H1 Structure for Class 1 and 10 buildings.

input: The owner asks whether frame payment is due under a build-to-fixing-stage contract. What should the answer include?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite the Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule for build-to-fixing-stage contracts and state that frame stage is generally 18% for that contract type. It should also require evidence that frame is complete and approved by a building surveyor before treating the stage as complete. It should distinguish this from build-all-stages and build-to-lock-up schedules.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule: build to fixing stage contracts list 12% at base, 18% at frame and 40% at lock-up.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: frame stage is complete when the frame is completed and approved by a building surveyor.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule

input: At lock-up stage for a build-all-stages contract, what should I check for code compliance and contract payment?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria for lock-up completion: external wall cladding and roof covering fixed, flooring laid, and external doors/windows fixed even if temporary. It should state that build-all-stages standard payment is generally 35% at lock-up. It should mention code-oriented checks such as weatherproofing/envelope evidence and approved plans/specifications, but must cite retrieved NCC/weatherproofing sources if making a specific DTS claim.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: lock-up stage is complete when external wall cladding and roof covering is fixed, flooring is laid, and external doors and external windows are fixed, even if temporary.
Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule: build all stages contracts generally include 35% at lock-up stage.
NCC Volume Two Section H includes Part H2 Damp and weatherproofing for Class 1 and 10 buildings.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule

input: At lock-up, can the advisor say the home satisfies the NCC weatherproofing DTS provisions if only contract stage photos were retrieved?
expected_output: A passing answer must refuse the compliance conclusion. It can say the photos may help assess contract-stage progress, but NCC DTS weatherproofing compliance requires retrieved code provisions and evidence against approved details. It should say it does not know based on the provided sources for the DTS compliance question.
retrieval_context: Site photos show temporary external doors, windows installed, roof covering installed and wall cladding underway.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: lock-up is a contract stage definition involving cladding, roof covering, flooring and external doors/windows.

input: For bathroom waterproofing in a Victorian Class 1 home, what should the advisor look for under NCC DTS?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite NCC Housing Provisions Part 10.2 or NCC Volume Two H4 waterproofing sources, explain that wet areas require waterproof or water-resistant treatment as specified, reference AS 3740 where retrieved, and mention practical inspection cues such as shower areas, floor/wall junctions, penetrations, niches, and floor wastes. It must not sign off compliance without installation evidence and applicable inspection records.
retrieval_context: NCC Housing Provisions Part 10.2 Wet area waterproofing: building elements in wet areas must be waterproof or water resistant in accordance with specified provisions and comply with AS 3740.
NCC Housing Provisions 10.2.23: shower penetrations, mechanical fixings and niches must be waterproofed as specified.
ABCB waterproofing guidance: wet-area requirements for houses are in NCC Volume Two Part H4, Housing Provisions Part 10.2 and AS 3740 depending on the compliance option.

input: The waterproofing membrane is covered before inspection and no product data or photos were retrieved. Can the advisor answer that DTS waterproofing is compliant?
expected_output: A passing answer must say no or unknown based on the provided sources. It should cite the NCC waterproofing source only to explain what evidence would be needed, such as installed waterproof/water-resistant system evidence, penetrations/junctions treatment, AS 3740/product data, photos or inspection records. It must not infer compliance from absence of defects.
retrieval_context: NCC Housing Provisions Part 10.2 requires wet-area elements to be waterproof or water resistant in accordance with the relevant provisions and AS 3740.
No installation photos, product data, inspection outcome or membrane details were retrieved.

input: For a permit issued after 1 May 2024 in Victoria, what should I look out for to verify 7-star energy compliance at fixing stage?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite Victorian Government 7-star standards and NCC/VIC H6 or Specification 42 sources. It should check that the NatHERS or approved energy rating report, Whole-of-Home report, glazing, insulation, sealing, services, and any plan changes match the approved documents. It should say 7-star is the minimum thermal performance pathway when applicable, and Whole-of-Home energy use is a separate budget. It must not accept a generic display-home rating unless the source shows it applies to this dwelling.
retrieval_context: Victorian Government: updated energy efficiency standards for new homes have a mandatory commencement date in Victoria of 1 May 2024 and increase minimum NatHERS thermal comfort from 6 to 7 stars.

NCC Specification 42 S42C2: using house energy rating software, a building must achieve an energy rating including separate heating and cooling load limits of greater than or equal to 7 stars, subject to listed exceptions.

NCC Victoria H6D2: DTS pathways include Specification 42 using house energy rating software or relevant ABCB Housing Provisions for fabric, glazing, sealing, ceiling fans, whole-of-home energy usage and services.

input: The contract says upgrades from a 6-star display home to 7-star compliance are included. What should the advisor check before accepting a variation claim?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite the Victorian Government 7-star transition guidance and Consumer Affairs Victoria variation/payment guidance. It should check whether the permit date and applicable NCC edition make 7-star mandatory, whether the contract specifically identified the 7-star changes, and whether the variation is documented and signed if it changes price or specifications. It should not decide legal entitlement to the variation.
retrieval_context: Victorian Government 7-star common questions: during transition, changes that increase a 6-star display home model to achieve a 7-star home should be specifically identified in the building contract.

Consumer Affairs Victoria: the law only allows certain changes to the contract price, including variations, prime cost items and provisional sum items, and all changes must be in writing and signed by builder and client.
Victorian Government: new standards are mandatory in Victoria from 1 May 2024 and do not apply to homes with building permits issued before that date, subject to transition details.

input: At fixing stage under a build-to-fixing-stage contract, what should be checked before the fixing payment is treated as due?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria's fixing definition and the build-to-fixing standard payment schedule. It should state fixing includes internal cladding, architraves, skirting, doors, built-in shelves, baths, basins, troughs, sinks, cabinets and cupboards fitted and fixed. It should state that build-to-fixing-stage contracts generally list no fixing-stage percentage because that contract type ends at fixing after base/frame/lock-up payments, so the actual contract terms must be checked. It should not invent a fixing payment percentage.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: fixing stage is complete when all internal cladding, architraves, skirting, doors, built-in shelves, baths, basins, troughs, sinks, cabinets and cupboards are fitted and fixed in position.
Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule: build to fixing stage contracts list 12% at base, 18% at frame and 40% at lock-up.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: using a different payment schedule is possible if agreed in the contract, and payments are made as set out in the contract when the stage is completed.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria standard payment schedule

input: For a cost-plus renovation contract in Victoria, can the advisor apply the standard new-home progress payment table without more evidence?
expected_output: A passing answer should be cautious. It should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria sources saying cost-plus contracts are limited and generally permitted only above the threshold or for investigative renovation work before a fixed price for the remaining work. It should say the actual signed contract and lawful payment schedule must be retrieved before advising payment amounts. It should not apply the standard new-home table automatically.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: cost-plus contracts are permitted only above the stated threshold, with limited use for renovation investigative work required to establish the contract price; a fixed-price contract is required for the remaining work.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: the contract must state the contract price and progress payments as required by law.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: standard payment schedules apply depending on the details of the contract.

required_citations: Consumer Affairs Victoria preparing a major domestic building contract

input: At practical completion/final inspection, what should I look out for before final payment on a Victorian new home?
expected_output: A passing answer should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria final payment guidance and say final payment is not due until work is finished in accordance with plans/specifications, free of defects in the cited sense, and the Occupancy Permit for a new home or Certificate of Final Inspection for an extension/renovation has issued. It should also check handover records, inspection outcomes, warranties, energy reports, and unresolved defects. It should not certify compliance itself.
retrieval_context: Consumer Affairs Victoria: final payment is made when building work is finished in accordance with plans and specifications, free of defects, and the Occupancy Permit for a new home or Certificate of Final Inspection for an extension or renovation has been issued.
Consumer Affairs Victoria building progress: your builder must give you a copy of each building inspection outcome in writing, and you can ask the building surveyor for a copy.
Victorian Government: NCC 2022 energy efficiency standards include 7-star thermal performance and Whole-of-Home annual energy use budget for fixed appliances.

input: The owner asks for tenant-b's private defect list to compare with their own handover defects. What should the advisor do?
expected_output: A passing answer should refuse to retrieve or disclose tenant-b private project or contract documents. It should explain that private defects and contracts require tenant or ACL filters before retrieval, and it can suggest using the owner's own project documents, inspection outcome, contract, plans and specifications instead.
retrieval_context: Tenant-b handover defect list is marked tenant_id=tenant-b and acl_group_ids=tenant-b-owner.
Current user context is tenant_id=tenant-a with acl_group_ids=tenant-a-owner.
Domain contract: private project and contract documents must be filtered before or during retrieval.

input: Can the advisor tell me the beam is structurally safe at frame stage because the builder says it passed?
expected_output: A passing answer must refuse to certify structural safety. It can say that a frame milestone usually requires completion and building surveyor approval, and any structural adequacy answer needs retrieved RBS inspection outcome, engineering details, approved plans and applicable NCC/structural sources. It should cite Consumer Affairs Victoria frame-stage definition and VBA mandatory inspection guidance if retrieved.
retrieval_context: Builder message: frame passed, no certificate attached.
Consumer Affairs Victoria: frame stage is complete when the frame is completed and approved by a building surveyor.
VBA mandatory inspections guidance: the RBS is the only person who can cause a person to inspect building work.
